food_subgroup: Cooked chicken dishes — stir-fried with aromatics

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of ginger and onion chicken is approximately 300 g, representing a shared zi char dish intended for 2–3 diners alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, cooked with ginger and spring onion — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA composite estimate for stir-fried chicken with aromatics
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research Malaysia (IMR) MyFCD; FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Tables; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (supplementary)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'ginger and onion chicken' as a named dish. Values are adapted from IMR MyFCD stir-fried chicken entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA cooked chicken with aromatics data, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy (NDB 05064, roasted chicken meat) for macro validation. Ginger, spring onion, soy sauce, and oyster sauce contributions are incorporated proportionally based on standard zi char recipe ratios. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-and-vegetable dishes — analogous estimate based on stir-fried chicken with vegetables; University of Sydney GI Database reference for low-GI protein-dominant dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for ginger and onion chicken. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous stir-fried chicken dishes in the University of Sydney GI Database, which consistently show GI values in the 30–40 range for protein-dominant, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken preparations. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 15.6 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 5.5, rounded to 6.1 accounting for sauce carbohydrates. gi_category is 'low' per the ≤55 threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce (~480 mg sodium per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–150 g and request reduced-sauce preparation, as a 300 g restaurant serving delivers approximately 1440 mg sodium, representing 60–72% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~5.2 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ginger and onion chicken has a low GI of approximately 35 and a low glycemic load, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed with a controlled portion of low-GI rice or vegetables; however, the sodium content from sauces warrants attention in those with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.3 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from cooking sauces
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, ginger and onion chicken should be consumed in moderation; the saturated fat content is moderate and manageable within a heart-healthy diet, but the high sodium from soy and oyster sauces poses a risk for blood pressure elevation and should be mitigated by requesting low-sodium sauce alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ginger and Onion Chicken (姜葱鸡, Jiang Cong Ji) is a classic Chinese-Malaysian stir-fried chicken dish widely served at zi char (煮炒) restaurants in Singapore and Malaysia. The dish features bite-sized pieces of chicken — typically bone-in or boneless thigh and drumstick meat — wok-fried at high heat with generous quantities of fresh ginger slices, spring onions (scallions), and onion wedges. The sauce base typically combines light soy sauce, oyster sauce, sesame oil, and sometimes Shaoxing rice wine, producing a savoury, slightly sweet, and aromatic glaze that coats the chicken. The texture of the chicken is tender and juicy with lightly caramelised edges, while the ginger provides a warming pungency and the spring onion contributes a fresh, herbaceous note. Nutritionally, the dish is protein-rich (~16.8 g protein per 100 g) with moderate fat (~9.4 g per 100 g) and low carbohydrate (~5.2 g per 100 g), yielding approximately 172 kcal per 100 g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is sodium content (~480 mg per 100 g) from the soy and oyster sauce components, which is relevant for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Ginger has recognised anti-inflammatory properties and may contribute modest benefits for metabolic health. The dish is typically served as part of a shared zi char meal alongside steamed white rice and vegetable dishes, and is a staple comfort food across Chinese-Malaysian and Singaporean households.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for ginger and onion chicken as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR MyFCD stir-fried chicken entries and FAO/INFOODS SEA cooked chicken data, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact dish-level data from a primary regional source is unavailable.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source databases used for this dish. All other -1 values in per_100g have been replaced with estimated numeric values from approved sources where possible. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for a typical zi char-style ginger and onion chicken preparation; actual values will vary by restaurant, recipe, and oil quantity used. The dish is served at Marina Seafood House - Boat Quay (field survey data, GD003229) at SGD 20.00. Sodium content is the primary clinical concern. GI is estimated at 35 (low, ≤55 threshold) based on analogous stir-fried chicken dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving (300 g) = (35 × 15.6 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 5.5–6.1. The dish is generally considered halal as it uses chicken and plant-based aromatics, though sauce ingredients should be verified per restaurant.
